enrichment.short_description: Free, open-source, self-hosted app for tracking ammunition inventory and logging shooting sessions.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Cannery is a self-hosted firearm and ammunition tracker built for shooters who want to keep tabs on their ammo supply without handing that data to a third party. Users log the ammunition they own, then record shooting sessions that automatically deduct rounds fired from the on-hand count, giving an accurate running total of remaining stock.

The project is open source (AGPLv3) and distributed as a Docker Compose deployment, so anyone comfortable running a small self-hosted service can stand up their own private instance, or use one hosted by someone they trust rather than a commercial provider.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who It's For
paragraphs: Cannery is aimed at individual firearm owners and reloaders who want a private, ad-free way to track ammunition levels and range usage, plus small shooting clubs or groups of friends who want to share a single self-hosted instance.

features.title: Ammunition inventory tracking
features.description: Log ammunition by type and caliber to keep a running count of what you have on hand.

features.title: Shooting session logging
features.description: Record range trips and automatically deduct rounds used from your tracked inventory.

features.title: Self-hosted deployment
features.description: Runs via Docker Compose, deployed behind your own reverse proxy on port 4000.

features.title: Privacy by design
features.description: Because it's self-hosted, ammunition data stays on infrastructure you or someone you trust controls.

features.title: Open-source codebase
features.description: Source is published under AGPLv3 and hosted on Codeberg for anyone to inspect, fork, or contribute to.

pros: Free and fully open source under AGPLv3
Keeps sensitive ammunition data under your own control
Simple Docker Compose deployment
Purpose-built for firearm/ammo tracking rather than generic inventory
cons: Requires self-hosting know-how (Docker, reverse proxy) to run
No official managed cloud hosting from the project itself
Narrow focus limited to ammunition, not general inventory or gear

faqs.question: Is Cannery free to use?
faqs.answer: Yes. Cannery is free, open-source software licensed under AGPLv3.

faqs.question: How do I host Cannery?
faqs.answer: Cannery ships as a Docker Compose setup; you run the container and reverse proxy to it on port 4000.

faqs.question: Does Cannery offer a hosted version?
faqs.answer: There's no official commercial cloud plan. Users self-host or use an instance run by someone they trust.

faqs.question: What does Cannery track?
faqs.answer: Ammunition inventory by caliber/type, plus shooting sessions that deduct rounds used from your stock.

use_cases.title: Personal ammo tracking
use_cases.description: Track how much ammunition you have left after each range trip.
use_cases.audience: Individual firearm owners and hobbyist shooters

use_cases.title: Shared club instance
use_cases.description: Run a single self-hosted Cannery instance for a small group or shooting club to track shared or individual stock.
use_cases.audience: Shooting clubs and groups of friends

enrichment.seo_meta_description: Cannery is a free, open-source, self-hosted app for tracking ammunition inventory and logging shooting sessions.

enrichment.short_description: Free, open-source, self-hosted inventory and organization system for tracking household belongings.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: HomeBox is a lightweight, self-hosted inventory and organization system built for home users. Written in Go with an embedded web UI, it's designed to be fast and easy to deploy, with idle memory usage typically under 50MB for the whole container.

This edition, maintained by SysAdminsMedia, is a community-driven continuation of the original HomeBox project by hay-kot, which stalled in 2024. SysAdminsMedia forked the codebase and resumed active development, and the software remains free and open source.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who It's For
paragraphs: HomeBox suits homeowners, renters, and hobbyists who want a private, searchable catalog of their belongings, warranties, and maintenance records instead of relying on spreadsheets.

features.title: Item organization
features.description: Organize belongings with categories, locations, and tags for fast filtering.

features.title: Powerful search
features.description: Search across your entire inventory to quickly find where an item is stored.

features.title: Image uploads
features.description: Attach photos to items for easier identification.

features.title: Document and warranty tracking
features.description: Store receipts, manuals, and warranty documents alongside each item.

features.title: Purchase and maintenance history
features.description: Track purchase details and maintenance events over an item's lifetime.

features.title: QR code generation
features.description: Generate QR labels for instant item lookup via the mobile web UI.

features.title: Bulk import/export
features.description: Use reusable item templates with CSV import and export for bulk data entry.

features.title: REST API
features.description: Automate inventory updates through a full REST API.

pros: Free and open source, no account or subscription required
Lightweight Go binary with a small memory footprint
Simple to self-host via Docker or as a bare-metal binary
Supports both SQLite (simple) and Postgres (larger installs)
Actively maintained community continuation after the original project stalled
cons: Self-hosting is required; there's no official managed cloud plan from the project
Focused on household/personal use rather than enterprise asset management
The community fork is relatively young, having resumed development in 2024

faqs.question: Is HomeBox free?
faqs.answer: Yes, HomeBox is free and open source.

faqs.question: How is this different from the original HomeBox project?
faqs.answer: The original project by hay-kot stalled in 2024; SysAdminsMedia forked it and continues active development.

faqs.question: What database does HomeBox use?
faqs.answer: SQLite by default for simplicity, with Postgres supported for larger deployments.

faqs.question: Can I deploy HomeBox without Docker?
faqs.answer: Yes, it can run as a bare-metal binary in addition to Docker deployment.

use_cases.title: Household inventory
use_cases.description: Catalog belongings, warranties, and receipts for a home.

use_cases.title: Shared equipment tracking
use_cases.description: Track shared gear and tools with locations and maintenance history.

enrichment.seo_meta_description: HomeBox (SysAdminsMedia) is a free, open-source, self-hosted inventory system for tracking home items, warranties, and documents.

enrichment.short_description: Open-source, non-profit platform for cataloging your books and sharing, lending, or giving them to friends and communities.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Inventaire lets people build an inventory of the books they own, mark which ones they're willing to give, lend, or sell, and choose who can see that inventory: the public, or just friends and specific groups. Book data is built on top of an open, multilingual knowledge graph connected to Wikidata, national libraries, and the fediverse rather than a closed proprietary catalog.

The project is stewarded by the Inventaire Association, a French non-profit created in 2019 to support full-time contributors, though the idea for the project dates back to 2011-2013. It is fully open source under AGPLv3, and the flagship instance runs at inventaire.io, with anyone free to run their own federated instance.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who It's For
paragraphs: Inventaire is used by individual book lovers who want to track and share their personal libraries, as well as by small collective-run libraries organized around specific topics or languages.

features.title: Personal book inventory
features.description: Catalog the books you own using entries linked to an open, Wikidata-backed bibliographic database.

features.title: Give, lend, or sell
features.description: Mark each book with what you're willing to do with it and to whom.

features.title: Visibility controls
features.description: Share your inventory publicly, or restrict it to friends and specific groups.

features.title: Community and group libraries
features.description: Used by collectives to run shared libraries around specific topics or languages.

features.title: Self-hostable federation
features.description: Anyone can run their own Inventaire instance in addition to the main inventaire.io site.

pros: Completely free, non-profit, and open source (AGPLv3)
Built on open bibliographic data rather than a closed proprietary catalog
Fine-grained control over who can see each book
Supports both individual and community/collective library use
cons: Focused narrowly on physical book sharing, not general inventory management
Self-hosting your own instance requires technical setup
Smaller community/network effect than mainstream book-cataloging apps

faqs.question: Is Inventaire free?
faqs.answer: Yes, it's a free, non-profit, open-source project.

faqs.question: Who runs Inventaire?
faqs.answer: The Inventaire Association, a French non-profit formed in 2019 to fund full-time contributors.

faqs.question: Can I self-host Inventaire?
faqs.answer: Yes, the software is open source and anyone can run their own federated instance.

faqs.question: How does Inventaire get its book data?
faqs.answer: It builds on an open, multilingual knowledge graph connected to Wikidata and other library sources.

use_cases.title: Personal library sharing
use_cases.description: Track your own books and let friends know what's available to borrow or take.

use_cases.title: Collective/topic libraries
use_cases.description: Run a shared library instance for a group organized around a topic or language.

enrichment.seo_meta_description: Inventaire is a free, open-source, non-profit platform for cataloging books and sharing, lending, or giving them to friends and communities.

enrichment.short_description: Open-source inventory management system for parts, stock control, and bill-of-materials tracking, built on Python/Django.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: InvenTree is an open-source inventory management system that provides parts management and stock control for small-to-medium businesses and hobbyists. It organizes parts into categories, links them to suppliers, tracks stock across cascading storage locations with serialization support, and supports bill-of-materials (BOM) upload/download plus build tracking for production runs.

Built on Python and Django, InvenTree is self-hosted and exposes a RESTful API with Python bindings, along with a plugin system for extending functionality. Official companion apps are available on the Google Play Store and Apple App Store for barcode scanning and mobile stock lookups. Third-party providers such as Elestio and Stellar Hosted offer managed InvenTree hosting, but the core project itself remains free and self-hosted.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who It's For
paragraphs: InvenTree fits small-to-medium businesses, electronics makers, and hobbyists who need flexible parts and stock tracking with BOM and production-build support, without the cost of a full enterprise ERP.

features.title: Parts organization
features.description: Categorize and structure parts for easy browsing and management.

features.title: Supplier management
features.description: Link suppliers to parts and track purchasing information.

features.title: Stock tracking
features.description: Track stock across cascading storage locations, with support for serialized items.

features.title: Bill of materials (BOM)
features.description: Upload and download BOMs in multiple formats for multi-level assemblies.

features.title: Build tracking
features.description: Monitor production builds and allocate components against them.

features.title: Custom reporting
features.description: Generate reports from customizable templates.

features.title: REST API and plugins
features.description: Integrate with other systems via a RESTful API and Python bindings, and extend behavior with plugins.

features.title: Official mobile apps
features.description: Native Android and iOS apps for barcode scanning and on-the-go stock lookups.

pros: Free and fully open source, self-hostable
Strong BOM and production-build tracking beyond simple stock counts
REST API and plugin system for customization and integration
Official native mobile apps for Android and iOS
Active development with third-party managed-hosting options available
cons: No official first-party cloud hosting; self-hosting or a third-party host is required
Requires familiarity with Python/Django-based deployments for advanced setups
Official mobile apps carry a separate one-time purchase price on app stores

faqs.question: Is InvenTree free?
faqs.answer: Yes, the core InvenTree software is free and open source.

faqs.question: Does InvenTree offer hosted cloud plans?
faqs.answer: Not directly from the project, but third parties such as Elestio and Stellar Hosted offer managed InvenTree hosting.

faqs.question: Is there a mobile app?
faqs.answer: Yes, official InvenTree apps are available on Google Play and the Apple App Store for barcode scanning and stock lookups.

faqs.question: Can InvenTree track bills of materials?
faqs.answer: Yes, it supports uploading/downloading BOMs and tracking production builds against them.

use_cases.title: Small business inventory
use_cases.description: Track parts, suppliers, and stock levels for a small manufacturing or electronics business.

use_cases.title: Hobbyist electronics inventory
use_cases.description: Organize personal component stock with BOMs for DIY builds.

enrichment.seo_meta_description: InvenTree is a free, open-source inventory management system for parts, stock control, BOMs, and production build tracking.

enrichment.short_description: Open-source, self-hosted inventory management system designed to scale from small retail to large warehouses.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Open QuarterMaster is an open-source inventory management system centered on a Core API that handles the generic tasks of tracking what's stored where. A frontend called the Base Station provides direct, easy-to-navigate access to inventory data, and a plugin architecture lets teams add capabilities such as advanced reporting or warehouse management on top of the core.

The project is maintained by Epic Breakfast Productions and developed in the open on GitHub, with documentation and a Discord community for support. It's built with no licensing fees or vendor lock-in, and is designed to scale from small retail operations to large distribution warehouses.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who It's For
paragraphs: Open QuarterMaster targets teams that need customizable, self-hosted inventory management at any scale, from small retailers to distribution centers, without paying for a proprietary system.

features.title: Core API
features.description: Central API that handles generic inventory tasks: what's stored, where, and related facts.

features.title: Base Station frontend
features.description: Web interface for direct, easy navigation of inventory managed by the Core API.

features.title: Plugin architecture
features.description: Extend the base system with plugins for advanced reporting, warehouse management, and more.

features.title: Scalable design
features.description: Architecture intended to adapt from small retail setups to large warehouse operations.

features.title: No vendor lock-in
features.description: Fully open-source with no licensing fees, so data and deployment stay under your control.

pros: Free and open source with no licensing fees
Modular plugin system for extending core functionality
Actively developed in the open on GitHub with community Discord support
Designed to scale from small businesses to large warehouses
cons: Requires self-hosting and technical setup (Debian-based deployment documented)
Smaller community compared to more established inventory platforms
Advanced modules like warehouse management depend on the plugin ecosystem maturing

faqs.question: Is Open QuarterMaster free?
faqs.answer: Yes, the software is free and open source; the project also sells optional branded merchandise separately.

faqs.question: Who maintains Open QuarterMaster?
faqs.answer: It's developed and maintained by Epic Breakfast Productions.

faqs.question: Can it scale to a warehouse?
faqs.answer: Yes, its modular design is intended to adapt from small retail to large warehouse operations.

faqs.question: How do I deploy it?
faqs.answer: The project's quickstart guide documents deployment on Debian-based systems.

use_cases.title: Small retail inventory
use_cases.description: Track stock for a small retail operation without a licensing cost.

use_cases.title: Warehouse/distribution inventory
use_cases.description: Extend the core system with plugins for warehouse-scale operations.

enrichment.seo_meta_description: Open QuarterMaster is a free, open-source, self-hosted inventory management system that scales from small retail to warehouses.

enrichment.short_description: Free, open-source, self-hosted inventory system for tracking and organizing electronic components.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Part-DB is a web-based, open-source inventory system purpose-built for tracking electronic components. It organizes parts by category, footprint, manufacturer, and storage location, supports barcode/label generation and scanning, and offers parametric search and filtering for finding components quickly.

The system includes project/BOM management, event logging with version history, two-factor authentication, and fine-grained user permissions with groups. It integrates with distributors such as Digikey, Mouser, and Octopart to auto-fill part information, and supports KiCad for electronics design workflows. Part-DB is licensed under AGPLv3 and can be deployed via Docker, direct installation on Debian, Kubernetes/Helm, or Proxmox VE LXC, backed by MySQL, SQLite, or PostgreSQL.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who It's For
paragraphs: Part-DB serves electronics hobbyists managing personal component stock, makerspaces with shared inventories, small companies, and educational institutions running electronics labs.

features.title: Component organization
features.description: Structure parts by category, footprint, manufacturer, and storage location.

features.title: Barcode and label support
features.description: Generate and scan barcodes/labels for physical component bins.

features.title: Distributor integration
features.description: Pull part data automatically from Digikey, Mouser, and Octopart.

features.title: KiCad integration
features.description: Connect with KiCad for electronics design workflows.

features.title: Project/BOM management
features.description: Track projects with bill-of-materials functionality.

features.title: User permissions and 2FA
features.description: Fine-grained group permissions with optional two-factor authentication.

features.title: Event logging and version history
features.description: Track changes to inventory over time.

features.title: REST API
features.description: Access and update inventory data programmatically via a RESTful API.

pros: Free and fully open source under AGPLv3
Purpose-built for electronic component tracking with distributor auto-fill
Multiple deployment options (Docker, Debian, Kubernetes/Helm, Proxmox LXC)
Multi-language interface (8 languages)
KiCad integration for hardware design workflows
cons: Requires self-hosting and database setup
Interface and workflow are specialized for electronics, not general inventory
Community-supported without a commercial support option

faqs.question: Is Part-DB free?
faqs.answer: Yes, it's completely free and open source under AGPLv3, supported by optional donations.

faqs.question: What databases does Part-DB support?
faqs.answer: MySQL, SQLite, and PostgreSQL.

faqs.question: Can Part-DB pull part data automatically?
faqs.answer: Yes, it integrates with Digikey, Mouser, and Octopart to fetch part information.

faqs.question: Does Part-DB integrate with KiCad?
faqs.answer: Yes, it offers integration for electronics design workflows in KiCad.

enrichment.short_description: Open-source equipment and asset management platform for tracking gear, bookings, and custody with QR codes.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Shelf (shelf.nu) is an open-source asset and equipment management platform built to replace spreadsheets for tracking gear. It lets teams tag assets with QR codes and labels, manage bookings and reservations to prevent scheduling conflicts, track custody of who currently holds an item, monitor asset locations, and run audits.

The code is open source and available on GitHub, and Shelf also offers a hosted product with a free personal tier and paid Plus, Team, and Enterprise plans. Native mobile apps are available for iOS and Android, and there's no per-user pricing on any plan.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who It's For
paragraphs: Shelf is used across universities, production companies and film studios, professional sports organizations, scientific research facilities, and utility/media companies that need to track physical equipment and prevent double bookings.

features.title: QR code asset tracking
features.description: Tag and track assets at scale using QR codes and printable labels.

features.title: Bookings and reservations
features.description: Reserve equipment and avoid double bookings with a booking calendar.

features.title: Custody management
features.description: Track who currently has custody of each asset for accountability.

features.title: Location tracking
features.description: Monitor where assets are stored or currently located.

features.title: Audits
features.description: Run equipment audits to verify inventory accuracy.

features.title: Mobile apps
features.description: Native iOS and Android apps for scanning and managing assets on the go.

features.title: Custom fields
features.description: Add custom fields to assets, unlimited on paid plans.

features.title: CSV import/export
features.description: Bulk import or export asset data on paid plans.

pros: Open-source codebase available on GitHub
Free tier with unlimited assets for a single user
No per-seat pricing on paid plans
Native mobile apps included on all plans
Purpose-built booking system to prevent equipment conflicts
cons: Bookings and reservations require the paid Team plan
Advanced features like SSO/SAML are Enterprise-only or an add-on
Self-hosting the open-source version requires your own setup versus using the hosted product

faqs.question: Is Shelf free?
faqs.answer: Yes, there's a free Personal plan with unlimited assets for one user; paid plans unlock team features.

faqs.question: Is Shelf open source?
faqs.answer: Yes, its code is available on GitHub and it can be self-hosted.

faqs.question: Does Shelf have a mobile app?
faqs.answer: Yes, native apps are available for iOS and Android.

faqs.question: Can Shelf handle equipment bookings?
faqs.answer: Yes, the Team and Enterprise plans include bookings, reservations, and a booking calendar.

use_cases.title: Media/production gear tracking

use_cases.title: Institutional asset management
use_cases.description: Manage shared equipment across departments with custody tracking.

enrichment.seo_meta_description: Shelf is an open-source asset management platform for tracking equipment, bookings, and custody with QR codes and mobile apps.

enrichment.short_description: Open-source, self-hosted headless CMS and real-time graph database built around the Atomic Data specification.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Atomic Server is an open-source headless CMS and real-time graph database for creating, sharing, and modeling structured data using the Atomic Data format. It's distributed as an 8MB, dependency-free binary that runs on Linux, Windows, macOS, and ARM devices, with sub-millisecond median response times.

It ships with a React-based Data Browser frontend, a RESTful API returning JSON-AD, full-text fuzzy search, real-time sync over websockets, event-sourced version history, an Ontology Editor for custom data models, Airtable-style table editing, collaborative documents, group chat, and AI integration via the MCP protocol. The project is MIT licensed, with JavaScript/TypeScript libraries (@tomic/lib, @tomic/react, @tomic/svelte), a Rust library, and a CLI tool. As of research, the project is in alpha status with breaking changes expected before a 1.0 release.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who It's For
paragraphs: Atomic Server is aimed at developers and teams building self-hosted, collaborative applications or structured-content systems who want a lightweight, real-time alternative to heavier headless CMS or graph database stacks.

features.title: Lightweight self-hosted binary
features.description: 8MB, dependency-free server that runs on Linux, Windows, macOS, and ARM.

features.title: RESTful API (JSON-AD)
features.description: Programmatic access to structured Atomic Data over a REST API.

features.title: Real-time sync
features.description: Websocket-based real-time synchronization across clients.

features.title: Ontology Editor
features.description: Define custom data models/schemas visually.

features.title: Table editing and documents
features.description: Airtable-style tables and collaborative documents built on the same data layer.

features.title: Full-text fuzzy search
features.description: Search across stored data with fuzzy matching.

features.title: Event-sourced versioning
features.description: Track and roll back changes using an event-sourced history model.

features.title: AI integration via MCP
features.description: Connect AI assistants to your data through the Model Context Protocol.

pros: MIT licensed and fully open source
Extremely lightweight and fast to self-host
Cross-platform binary (Linux, Windows, macOS, ARM)
Real-time collaboration features (tables, docs, chat) built in
Native AI/MCP integration for connecting assistants to structured data
cons: Still in alpha, with breaking changes expected before 1.0
Atomic Data's graph-based model has a learning curve versus traditional relational CMS tools
Smaller ecosystem/community than mainstream headless CMS platforms

faqs.question: Is Atomic Server free?
faqs.answer: Yes, it's free and open source under the MIT license.

faqs.question: What platforms does it run on?
faqs.answer: Linux, Windows, macOS, and ARM devices, as a single lightweight binary.

faqs.question: Is Atomic Server production-ready?
faqs.answer: It's currently in alpha status, and breaking changes are expected before a 1.0 release.

faqs.question: Does it support AI integration?
faqs.answer: Yes, it integrates with AI assistants via the Model Context Protocol (MCP).

use_cases.title: Real-time collaborative data apps

enrichment.seo_meta_description: Atomic Server is a free, open-source, self-hosted headless CMS and real-time graph database with API and AI/MCP integration.

enrichment.short_description: Free, open-source, self-hosted knowledge base application for building documentation and support sites.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: LibreKB is a self-hosted, PHP-based knowledge base web application for creating and managing documentation or support content. It includes user management with predefined groups, a TinyMCE-based article editor, email-based password resets, and a responsive Bootstrap interface with customizable branding.
The project is 100% free and open source under the GPL-3.0 license, runs on MySQL, and can be deployed on nearly any standard web host or via Docker, without vendor lock-in.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who It's For
paragraphs: LibreKB fits organizations and small businesses that want an affordable, self-hosted alternative to commercial knowledge base or help-center software.

features.description: Manage contributors and readers with predefined user groups.

features.description: Built-in email-based password recovery for user accounts.

features.title: Custom branding
features.description: Customize the look and branding of your knowledge base.

features.title: Responsive design
features.description: Bootstrap-based UI that works across desktop and mobile browsers.

features.title: Flexible deployment
features.description: Runs on standard PHP/MySQL hosting or via Docker.

No vendor lock-in versus commercial knowledge base SaaS tools
Customizable branding for internal or public-facing documentation
cons: Requires self-hosting and basic server administration
Smaller feature set than mature commercial knowledge base platforms
No official managed cloud hosting from the project

enrichment.short_description: Free, open-source e-learning platform used to deliver online courses, training, and certifications.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Chamilo LMS is a free, open-source e-learning platform used to deliver online courses, training programs, certifications, and events. The project was founded in 2010 as a fork intended to protect the openness of an earlier free-software e-learning platform, and its codebase traces back further to the Claroline project first published in 2001.

Chamilo is recognized as a Digital Public Good by the UN-backed Digital Public Goods Alliance and is GDPR compliant. It has been governed by BeezNest, a Belgium-based open-source software company, since 2015 (and specifically Belgium since January 2026); BeezNest has produced roughly 90% of the platform's code. Chamilo reports over 41 million learners worldwide using the platform.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who It's For
paragraphs: Chamilo is used by educational institutions, training organizations, and enterprises that need a free, self-hostable LMS for course delivery, certification, and online events.

features.title: Online course delivery
features.description: Build and deliver structured online courses to learners.

features.title: Certifications
features.description: Issue training certifications on course completion.

features.title: Online events
features.description: Host and manage online learning events within the platform.

features.title: GDPR compliance
features.description: Built with GDPR compliance for handling learner data in the EU.

features.title: Community support
features.description: Access community forums and a training catalog for support and learning resources.

pros: Free and open source with no licensing cost
Recognized as a UN Digital Public Good
GDPR compliant, relevant for EU-based institutions
Large existing user base (41M+ reported learners)
Backed by a dedicated company (BeezNest) actively developing the platform
cons: Self-hosting requires server setup and maintenance
Fewer built-in integrations than some commercial LMS platforms
Support beyond community resources may require BeezNest's paid services

faqs.question: Is Chamilo LMS free?
faqs.answer: Yes, Chamilo is free, open-source software.

faqs.question: Who develops Chamilo?
faqs.answer: BeezNest, a Belgium-based open-source software company, has governed and produced most of Chamilo's code since 2015.

faqs.question: When was Chamilo founded?
faqs.answer: The Chamilo project was founded in 2010, building on code originating from the Claroline project (2001).

faqs.question: Is Chamilo GDPR compliant?
faqs.answer: Yes, the platform is GDPR compliant.

faqs.question: Is Chamilo recognized by any standards bodies?
faqs.answer: Yes, it's certified as a Digital Public Good by the UN-backed Digital Public Goods Alliance.
